data.title: Acadia Healthcare: Earnings Selloff Surprising, But Now Is Not The Time To Buy
data.description:
Context Ahead Of Q2 Earnings Discussion - Stock Tanks 75% in 12 Months

Acadia Healthcare Company, Inc. (NASDAQ:ACHC), a leading provider of behavioral healthcare services across the United States, announced its Q2 2025 earnings and business updates yesterday, triggering a market sell-off that saw the company's shares lose >15% of their value.

Pre-market today, stock trades at $18, down >50% year-to-date, and down 75% on a 12-month basis, albeit still up from its 2011 IPO price of $7.5 - by ~140%.

In my last note on Acadia, shared with Seeking Alpha readers in late September last year, the company's stock was in free-fall as it had disclosed that it had received a request for information and a jury subpoena from a U.S. Attorney and jury subpoena in two jurisdictions related to its admissions, length of stay and billing practices.

I wrote in that note:

The disclosures come a day after the Acadia and the Department of Justice reached a ~$20M agreement to settle accusations that the company billed Medicare, Medicaid, and TRICARE for medically unnecessary inpatient mental health services.

Acadia has found itself under pressure after a New York Times investigation published earlier in September allegedly found that the company kept patients in facilities against their will to maximize insurance payments.

In that note I also gave Acadia stock a "Hold" rating, concluding:

In my view, what seems to be the case is that the larger Acadia grows, the greater its influence over the mental health landscape becomes, and with that brings greater responsibility, and greater scrutiny, which is impacting on Acadia's share price.

Until this conundrum is resolved satisfactorily, with concrete evidence that Acadia and its senior management team have the situation under control, I doubt that Wall Street is going to make substantial investments in the company's stock based on "good but not great" credentials.

I suspect the situation will take time to resolve, so I am firmly on the side-lines, and I doubt there is a "buy the dip" contrarian opportunity in play here.

Hold was probably too optimistic a call, as Acadia stock kept falling, briefly staging a recovery in January, after a couple of analysts upgraded their ratings on the company, one suggesting an "attractive risk reward," at $40 per share, but the stock tanked again after announcing Q4 2024 earnings, which missed on revenues and earnings per share ("EPS").

For the full-year 2024, Acadia reported revenues of $3.2bn, and EPS of $2.79, up from $2.39bn, and $(0.24) in 2023, and guided for full-year 2025 revenues of $3.3bn - $3.4bn, adjusted EBITDA of $675m - $725m, and adjusted EPS of $2.5 - $2.8, and total new bed additions of 800 - 1,000.

As I shared in my last note, and as we can see below, Acadia has traditionally been a growing and profitable company:

Given its current market cap of $1.66bn, we might consider that a forward price to sales ratio of ~0.5x, and forward price to earnings ratio of ~7x are low enough to support a contrarian trade thesis, or conclude that there are clearly serious issues at the company responsible for driving the share price so low. In order to determine which of these theses is correct, let's dive into Q2 earnings.

Acadia Healthcare - Q2 Earnings Overview

First, let's consider the headline figures as shared via press release yesterday. Revenue in Q2 came to $869.2m, which was up 9.2% year-on-year, with same facility revenue up up 9.5% year-on-year. Net income came to $30.1m, or $0.33 per diluted share, while adjusted income was reported as $74.8m, or $0.83 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA came to $201.8m, up 7.6% year-on-year. 101 new beds were added to existing facilities during the quarter.

Updated full-year guidance was for $3.3bn - $3.35bn of revenues, adjusted EPS of $2.25 - $2.65, and adjusted EBITDA of $675m - $700m. The company beat on revenues (according to Seeking Alpha), and on adjusted EPS, missing on GAAP EPS.

According to Acadia's quarterly report / 10Q filing:

We are committed to providing the communities we serve with high-quality, cost-effective behavioral healthcare services, while growing our business, increasing profitability and creating long-term value for our stockholders. This strategy includes five growth pathways: expansions of existing facilities, joint venture partnerships, de novo facilities, acquisitions and expansion across our continuum of care.

At June 30, 2025, we operated 274 behavioral healthcare facilities with approximately 12,100 beds in 39 states and Puerto Rico. During the six months ended June 30, 2025, we added 479 beds, consisting of 191 beds added to existing facilities and 288 beds added through the opening of one wholly-owned facility and one joint venture facility, and we opened eleven CTCs.

In terms of debt, Acadia reported a cash position of $131.4m, with $409m accounts receivable, $20.3m of near-term debt, and $2.25bn of long-term debt. The company generated $133.5 of cash from operations in Q2.

In summary, this doesn't necessarily feel like a set of earnings that would send a company's stock price plunging. If anything, it seems to support the argument that Acadia's business is undervalued. On its earnings call with analysts yesterday, management noted that:

For the second quarter, we added 101 beds to existing facilities, bringing the total to 191 beds added to existing facilities for the first half of 2025. Including the 288 beds from newly constructed facilities, we have added a total of 479 beds to date in 2025.

On a more negative note, on the earnings call company CEO and Director Christopher Howal Hunter told analysts that "same-facility patient days increased by 1.8%, which was slightly below our expectations," and that "we did observe a deterioration in performance at one facility, which continues to face particularly strong local market pressures," and also that "we experienced pockets of weakness in volumes in certain acute care markets with higher Medicaid exposure."

Analysis - Acadia Comments On DoJ, SEC Investigations

In response to an analysts' question regarding spending on government investigations, on yesterday's earnings call, the CEO responded as follows:

as we've previously communicated, we're committing to and have been committed to conducting a very thorough and independent review of our operations while continuing to work very cooperatively with the DOJ and the SEC. And while the pace of the government investigations and the related internal reviews that we're doing are going to naturally ebb and flow, much of the independent review and the cooperative engagement with the government has been performed in the first half of the year.

Acadia's departing Chief Financial Officer added:

I would just pick up on the last part of your question there around whether there are any settlement costs in there.

In its 10Q filing, Acadia confirms SEC and DoJ investigations are ongoing, adding that:

At this time, the Company cannot speculate on whether the outcome of these investigations will have any impact on its business or operations and cannot reasonably estimate the amount or range of the ultimate liability, if any, in connection with these investigations.

Acadia also notes that it paid $19.9m in 2024 "for the release and discharge of any civil or administrative monetary claims arising from the 2017 OIG/DOJ Investigation."

In summary, while the investigations are ongoing, there is clearly some jeopardy, and significant reputational damage has been done, however, in strictly financial terms, it's arguably harder to make the case that these investigations will be overly damaging to Acadia's business in the long-term, at least based on the outcome of previous investigations.

Legal costs in the first half of 2025 have apparently been in the region of $85m, and the resignation of the company CFO is notable, but we may conclude the investigations are not solely the reason for Acadia's dismal share price performance in 2025 to date.

Is The "Big Beautiful Bill" Weighing Down Acadia's Share Price?

The "Big Beautiful Bill" is anything but for some ~12m Americans who may face losing their Medicaid health insurance by 2034, and it is also problematic for companies like Acadia, who will have to absorb more of the cost if treating patients, it's speculated.

Americans eligible for Medicaid will have to prove they are working, volunteering, or attending school in order to qualify for insurance, which in the case of mental health patients, would presumably be difficult to do. Affordable Care Act ("ACA") policyholders will be required to update income, immigration status, and other data on an annual basis, also.

Nevertheless, Acadia's CEO suggested the company would not feel too much impact from the Bill, commenting that:

We believe the provisions of the bill are manageable over the coming years particularly due to the carve-outs from work requirements and the extended time line for implementing changes to the supplemental payment provisions of the Medicaid program.

For the full year 2025, we expect gross revenue of approximately $230 million from existing state Medicaid supplemental programs. More than half of this revenue comes from states that may begin reducing these payments starting in fiscal 2028 if proposed changes to the programs are implemented.

If these changes occur, we also anticipate that a portion of the revenue loss would be offset by a reduction in the provider taxes we pay in those states.

Regarding the Medicaid work requirements included in the legislation, we do not expect a material impact on our operations as these begin to be phased in next year. This is largely due to exemptions for the populations we serve, including individuals with chronic substance use disorders and those with serious and complex medical conditions.

Again, I find myself broadly in agreement with Acadia's CEO that, given policy changes will not fully hit until 2028, and given exemptions available, the Bill should not necessarily weigh too heavily on the company's valuation.

Concluding Thoughts - Growing + Profitable = Undervalued? Potentially, But I Remain On Sidelines

Acadia's management is clearly focused on growing the business, noting in a recent corporate presentation that ~30m Americans with mental illness receive no treatment, and that ~75k new beds are required in the U.S. to meet optimal demand, and a forecast annual 33% increase in deaths by suicide between 2009 - 2033.

In the same presentation Acadia notes that it earns 57% of its revenues from Medicaid, however, so perhaps the market is right to be spooked by underperformance in this sector in Q2, and the threats posed by the "Big, Beautiful Bill."

As we can see above (I shared a similar slide in my last note) Acadia works with numerous healthcare organizations and is able to point to data suggesting it offers a premium quality service, as below:

As such, we can make a case that Acadia stock is undervalued and presents an attractive contrarian trade idea - even when downgrading Acadia stock recently, on analyst at Barclays has set a share price target of $35, implying >100% upside, based on current share price.

Metrics such as P/S and P/E also suggest undervaluation, however I would continue to preach caution. Softness in the Acadia Healthcare Company, Inc. Medicaid business was evident in Q2, even if not too material to overall performance, and the long-term outlook given proposed changes to insurance policy should be considered a potential threat, even if management remain positive.

Reputationally, Acadia still seems to have a mountain to climb, and with sentiment apparently against the company, reflected in the undervaluation of the business, we should not necessarily expect this to be reversed quickly.

To conclude, while I am surprised Acadia stock has lost as much value as it has since my last note, reading the runes, I'd caution investor's to be patient, as there are clearly more sellers than buyers in the market for Acadia stock presently, and I am not convinced we have hit the bottom yet.

data.title: Tiny Tech, Huge Deal: Trident's Bold Pivot Could Pay Off
data.description:
Trident Digital Tech Holdings Ltd. (TDTH) has been public for almost a year, and the business is undergoing meaningful changes. It started as a business consulting and marketing agency, where they provided a full business plan that included market analysis, strategic planning, rebranding, and personalized marketing strategies, also offering technical solutions with system integration. However, the company is now shifting its focus toward more scalable strategies. They are building a blockchain-based Web 3 e-commerce platform called Tridentity, which aims to outperform other e-commerce platforms by enabling better third-party integration and by decentralizing the consumer experience, giving sellers more control over their storefronts. However, the biggest short-term catalyst is a partnership with the Democratic Republic of Congo [DRC] to develop the country's entire digital identity system. But before I explain it, I think it is better to look at the company’s financial results to better understand the potential impact of these new initiatives.

Finances Are Weak, as Expected

As a company in the growth phase, it is expected that capital is going to be scarce. However, Trident has accumulated a shareholders’ deficit of $381,062, compared to positive shareholders’ equity of $2.43 million a year earlier, meaning that the company’s total liabilities are greater than their total assets, as total assets are close to $3 million while total liabilities are $3.4 million.

The cash position also declined significantly, from $1.8 million in 2023 to $194,113 in 2024. Accounts receivable in 2024 were just $80,587, much lower than accounts payable, $322,027. Yet, on a positive note, current assets are still exceeding current liabilities, $1.66 million versus $1.37 million, respectively, but this cushion is not enough to sustain operations for another full year, given the operational deficit we will see shortly in the income statement.

However, to safeguard the next year’s operations, according to the company’s 2024 10-K:

“On April 17, 2025, Mr. Soon Huat Lim, the ultimate controller of the Group, provided a financial support letter to the Group, pursuant to which up to SGD 8.0 million cash can be further provided to meet the Group’s other liabilities and commitments as they become due for at least twelve months.”

This financial backing signals management’s confidence in their strategy going forward. However, the company will still need further financial assistance to scale its new initiatives as losses continue to grow.

On the income statement, revenues dropped sharply, from $1.4 million in 2023 to $0.47 million in 2024 because, as the company is prioritizing its new e-commerce platform, the consulting business had fewer resources. Cost of revenues declined alongside revenues, from $1.2 million in 2023 to $0.46 million in 2024. General and administrative expenses had the largest impact on the net result; they went from $0.9 million in 2022 to $3.9 million in 2023 to $6.4 million in 2024. This increase was largely due to higher staff costs and a one-time $2.6 million bonus for executive officers, which could raise concerns, but we don’t know for sure what was behind this bonus; perhaps it was the successful negotiations of that deal with the DRC.

The company’s bottom line continued to weaken, from negative $1.2 million in 2022 to negative $4.8 million in 2023 to negative $8.1 million in 2024. Nonetheless, regarding the poor financial results, the company gave us a hint of what they are expecting for 2025 on the 2024’ 10-K:

“The Group is pursuing private financing of debt or equity and developing new business in 2025, which the Group believes will generate sufficient profit and cash to support the Group’s operation. The Group believes the private financing and the new business may help to alleviate liquidity pressure.”

To me, this suggests the company is confident that the deal with the DRC will sustain the business while the e-commerce application is being developed.

The deal with the Democratic Republic of Congo [DRC]

In August 2024, the DRC canceled a civil registry implementation with the French company Afritech after the World Bank rejected financing it because of a large contract amount, $1.2 billion, as it would be close to the most expensive ID projects in the world, but also due to the DRC’s reputation for corruption, ranked 151st out of 180 countries on the 2024’ Corruption Perceptions Index.

In December 2024, Trident announced an agreement with the DRC to develop and implement the country's digital identity system, integrating it with various sectors such as banking, healthcare, and the tax system, and on June 25, 2025, they signed the definitive public-private partnership [PPP] agreement. The application will be built in blockchain technology through the Tridentity app, which will also bring users to the e-commerce side of the platform.

This is a major opportunity for the company. The previous project, which had a $1.2 billion price tag, showed how much the country is willing to invest in this project, and, while Trident has not announced the contracted amount, we can compare it to other countries' projects, such as Australia and the European Union, which had significantly lower budgets, averaging $70 million. But we also need to factor that the DRC has a fragmented identification system, and this project is going to be a lot more relevant for the DRC, also more complete than those in Australia or the EU.

So, given these factors, it is fair to estimate that this project contract value could range from $50 million to $500 million. Even if its budgeted close to low end, $50 million, it would still be very positive for the company that has a $3.5 million total liabilities on the balance sheet and is operating at an $8 million annual net loss. Meaning that, in the most conservative case, the company will have at least 5 years to build their Tridentity app. But if it is close to the higher end, which I believe is likely, then the stock could be significantly undervalued at current levels. Additionally, the DRC has 110 million inhabitants, over 80 million mobile users, that would become users of the e-commerce platform with the backing of the government.

Following the confirmation of the partnership with DRC, Trident’s management has shown increased confidence by announcing a $1 million share repurchase program and a $500 million XRP treasury strategy with a goal of integrating the digital token with their applications, as XRP offers a low fee and it is already used by many financial institutions for cross-border payments. This is another layer in the investment thesis, bringing both strategic synergy with their platform and potential token appreciation. But it also increases the risk and volatility of the investment, as it will be very exposed to the cryptocurrency price fluctuations.

Risks

There are several risks related to this company. First, it is shifting its core operation to a much more complex business model, and its team may lack the experience to execute at scale it.

The e-commerce space is highly competitive, and the company could struggle to gain market share. Additionally, Trident is building in a recent technology, Web3, that could become harder to scale in the future as more users come to the platform.

The partnership with the DRC also carries significant political risk due to the country’s history of corruption. There are also regulatory risks related to the handling of personal data and the company’s use of Chinese infrastructure, which could limit adoption in certain regions of the world.

Lastly, the use of XRP, as part of its treasury strategy, exposes the company to elevated volatility.

Conclusion

Trident is taking a bold bet on emerging technology that, if successful, could become an industry standard, but has yet to prove itself.

However, the DRC partnership is a major win for the company, as it will help them fund the app’s development but also bring millions of new users with the institutional validation.

Given this significant development, I rate the stock a BUY, as the potential value of the DRC contract could far exceed the company’s current costs, providing meaningful upside.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apollo Global Q2: Record High Earnings, Strong Momentum
data.description:
Our second quarter results reflect the strength of Apollo’s business model and the discipline with which we operate... The reward for good performance is inflows.

Marc Rowan, CEO of Apollo.

I rate Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) a buy, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. Apollo is a global leader in alternative asset management and retirement services, which is on an extraordinary track to growth.

Q2 results proved the power of “the Apollo business model.” It achieved record results in all earning segments, including Fee, Spread, and Principal. Apollo has unique strengths that help them capture Alpha where others can’t and may charge a higher fee. Not only was Q2 a record-breaking quarter, momentum going into Q3 is equally strong.

Apollo’s outlook is equally extraordinary. As financial advisors are moving more client assets to passive index funds, the one area they can earn higher fees is selling alternative assets such as private investments and leveraging retirement insurance plans. One of the major catalysts to Apollo’s growth in the future is a change in regulations that would allow alternative investments in retirement plans. A change in this regulation (which is likely) would expand the market for Apollo to offer their products to a much larger market.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are

  • Stock value total return outperforms the S&P500 and Financial Sector over the past 10 years.
  • Top-notch governance with high-performing teams.
  • High-growth potential in addressable markets such as alternative assets, retirement, and international opportunities.
  • Potential change in financial regulations, allowing Apollos products to be held in retirement accounts.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold 2.92 (as of 8/6/25). Valuation is challenged due to the stock’s high premium vs. the sector.

Global Alternative Asset Manager

Headquartered in New York, Apollo is a high growth, global alternative asset manager, with three primary business segments: Asset Management, Retirement Services, and Principal investing. The company serves high net worth individual investors, institutions, pension funds, and sovereign wealth funds. The retirement services business operates under Athene, which specializes in helping clients achieve financial security by providing a suite of retirement savings products and acting as a solutions provider to institutions. As of June 30, 2025, Apollo had $840 billion of assets under management.

According to the May Investor Presentation, Apollo has:

  • #1 Alternative Credit Business.
  • #1 Investment Grade Alternative Credit Business (over 60% investment grade).
  • #1 US Annuity Sales.
  • 35 years in Private Equity – IRR since inception 24% net.
  • $785 billion in AUM for Apollo Asset Management.
  • $31 billion in Regulatory Capital for Athene Retirement Services.

Q2 Earnings Results

On August 5, 2025, the company announced Q2 earnings results for the period ending June 30, 2025, with the following financial highlights:

  • Fee related earnings up +22% to $627 million.
  • Spread related earnings up +16% to $821 million.
  • Principal investing income up 42% to 47 million.
  • Segment income up +19% to $1,495 million.
  • Total Adjusted net income grew +17% to $1,179 million.
  • Total AUM of $840 billion with inflows of $61 billion in Q2.
  • Declared cash dividend of $0.51 per share, goes ex-dividend on 8/18.

Beyond the stellar earnings report, APO also executed well on three strategic growth pillars. First, on origination, APO achieved record quarterly origination activity of $81 billion from debt and core credit. Second, Global Wealth had strong quarterly inflows of $4 billion. Third, Capital solutions achieved record quarterly fee revenue of $216 million.

APO also highlighted ways in which they drove shareholder value by allocating capital. $170 million was invested in various investments. The board approved share repurchases of $1.3 billion of common stock over the last 12 months.

Accelerating Performance

A $10,000 investment in APO in the past 10 years outperforms both the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Financial Sector (XLF) in a highly dramatic way, growing the investment to $114,100.

Valuation

Given APO’s extraordinary growth, the stock price commands a premium. APO is currently trading at a price earnings ratio of 25.4 on a forward basis compared to the sector median of 12.6. Similarly, the price to book ratio is 2.4, a premium over the sector at 1.1. Finally, the dividend yield is lower at 1.3% vs. sector at 3.1%, again highlighting the price premium of APO vs. the sector.

Over the past 10 years, APO has offered a quarterly dividend which has fluctuated with earnings.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, APO is performing well. The 10-year total return tells the story best, showing a 1,046% growth in the stock price. Amongst peers, APO is less than half the size of the largest competitor, with unique product offerings such as retirement insurance products under the Athene brand name. Its size shows just how large it could be over time.

Blackstone Inc. (BX) is the industry leader in alternative investments with a market cap of $208 billion. With a 279% 5-year total return and 28.8% ROE, it's not surprising to see a premium price to book ratio of 15.7. The category leader usually is priced at a premium because they have a record of accomplishment of success. In my view, this is where APO is heading.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) has the highest 5-year total return of 318%. Many of its products are riskier than their competitors, in my view, since they use a fair amount of leverage.

The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG) is the smallest of the group at only $22 billion in market capitalization. The stock has the lowest 5-year total return of 163%, which is very high, showing the strength of this category.

Over the past 10 years, APO has outperformed its peers in total return. This growth has come from a small company becoming much larger, accelerating through acquisitions such as Athene retirement products. Based on their current momentum, I can see APO passing KKR to challenge BX. In the chart below, note how APO outperformed competitors over 10 years, acting more like a tech company vs. a financial services company, in my view.

Outlook

APO competes in highly attractive markets with unique capabilities. For example, the retirement market is estimated at $45 trillion. While APO currently offers insurance products through Athene and is growing exponentially, the market is on track to widen further through the advancement of regulations allowing alternative investments to be held in retirement accounts. In addition, as financial advisors turn to alternatives for active management, APO offers products to address these needs. Again, regulation is pending to make these private investments more widely available.

While Marc Rowan was not selected to be Treasury Secretary, shareholders should rejoice that he stayed at Apollo, in my view. His leadership will propel the company to new heights.

Risks To Rating

The largest opportunity, therefore, the largest risk to APO’s outlook is the regulation allowing alternative investments into retirement plans. If the political climate prevents this from happening, the company is still on a growth trajectory.

The next largest risk is a slowdown in the macroeconomy, such as a recession. During these times, investors will slow their appetite for risk, and individuals may put off retirement after seeing their investments lose value.

Bottom Line

I rate APO as a buy for capital appreciation-focused investor looking for long-term buy and hold investments. Q2 results showed growth in fee-related income in all key segments, achieving record results. The stock’s 10-year total return is over 1,000%, reaching tech stock status for extraordinary growth.

APO has unique strengths vs. peers. As the company is less than half the size of Blackstone, it still manages to lower costs through scale and attract inflows through performance. APO is the only financial services company amongst peers that offers retirement insurance products, building a strength that few can match.

The management team at APO, led by Marc Rowan, is one of the best in the world, in my view, as demonstrated by a long history of results.

The outlook for APO is very attractive. Their performance in investments is attracting inflows, which grows the AUM, and the nature of their product offerings is becoming in greater demand as well. Financial advisors are turning to alternative investments for active management products, and within government there is discussion of changing regulations to allow alternatives in retirement plans, which would be a massive opportunity for APO.

data.title: Will Next Week's Consumer Inflation Report Highlight Tariff Risk?
data.description:
President Trump’s new round of “reciprocal” tariffs kicks in today, a shift that focuses attention anew on forecasts by many economists that the levies on imports will raise inflation, if only temporarily. The outlook will be stress-tested with next week’s July data for the consumer price index (CPI), set for release on Tues., Aug. 12.

Beth Hammack, president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland, earlier this week said that inflation could tick higher this year as businesses start to pass on the cost of higher tariffs to consumers through price hikes.

“Individual businesses have been trying to hold back on, passing on those [tariff] costs, because they’re worried about what it might mean for demand,” she advised. “They’ve been selling inventory that they’d accumulated early in the year, and so they didn’t need to charge for the tariffs on that, but they’re coming through those stockpiles.”

Next week’s CPI update will be keenly read for signs on whether that concern is resonating. For some perspective on what the report may contain, let’s review the data published to date, followed by a short summary of expectations. Start with year-over-year CPI through June. As shown in the chart below, headline and core CPI edged higher to 2.7% and 2.9% year-over-year rates, respectively. The slightly firmer reading marks the first back-to-back increases for the annual pace of inflation for both series in more than three years. In absolute terms, however, it’s still reasonable to assume that the overall inflation rate hasn’t changed much.

The Capital Spectator will focus on core CPI data (excluding volatile food and energy prices), which offers a more reliable measure of the trend vs. the noisier headline numbers. Our ensemble model forecasts no change in core CPI change in July vs. the year-ago level, based on the point forecast.

Betting markets are currently leaning into expectations that June’s year-over-year increase for headline CPI will remain more or less steady. Data at Kalshi points to annual inflation holding at 2.7%.

The outlook via Polymarket favors a fractionally higher inflation rate for July at a 2.8% year-over-year change.

Many analysts advise that tariff-related inflation will take time to show up in the data. Based on current expectations for next week’s CPI numbers, the wait looks set to continue beyond next week’s CPI update in the search for meaningful signs of hotter price effects.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Airbnb: Bookings Re-Acceleration Makes Me Hopeful (Upgrade)
data.description:
The market has been tough to please during the Q2 earnings season, and even "beat and raise" quarters are being met with harsh skepticism as stocks try to defend sharp valuation premiums. Amid this challenging backdrop, investors need to deploy careful stock-picking in order to protect our portfolios from potential downside as we move into the back half of 2025.

Airbnb, Inc. (NASDAQ:ABNB) is one of the latest victims of investor backlash despite good results. The company reported a sharp acceleration in both revenue growth and bookings, driven largely by higher-ticket reservations. Still, the stock fell more than 5% in after-hours trading as investors were unimpressed by the company's bookings guidance for Q3.

I last wrote a bearish note on Airbnb in June, when the stock was still trading at similar ~$130 levels. Since then, Airbnb has achieved a re-acceleration in bookings across most of its key markets, and a pledge that this strength is poised to continue in Q3. The company has also regained its momentum in margin expansion, capitalizing on higher-ticket bookings. Amid the company's planned product expansions to capture more of its customers' wallet share, I'm upgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

To me, at current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for Airbnb. On the positive side for this company:

  • Airbnb is growing its product portfolio to capture more wallet share. The company launched Services in May, which allows guests to book experiences like an in-home chef and massages. The company is also integrating additional products like travel insurance at checkout, which is helping to boost the company's take rates.
  • Rich adjusted EBITDA margins. The company is targeting a rich mid-30s adjusted EBITDA margin for the year, as increased revenue take rates and a modest pace of opex expansion are allowing Airbnb to capture significant economies of scale.

At the same time, however, we have to be cognizant of several key risks:

  • Travel demand remains shaky. Though the company has noted some evidence of a bookings rebound in North America, a cautious consumer spending environment may cause very choppy bookings trends through the remainder of the year.
  • Sharp competition. Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG), the largest online travel agency (OTA), in particular, is leaning in on its apartment-style listings to compete more effectively with Airbnb, while Expedia Group, Inc. (EXPE) also has Vrbo as a direct answer to Airbnb. Both of Airbnb's main rivals tout a rewards program, which Airbnb does not.

From a valuation standpoint, Airbnb trades at fair - but not exactly attractive - multiples. At current share prices near $130, Airbnb commands a market cap of $80.55 billion. After netting off the $10.61 billion of cash and $1.99 billion of debt on Airbnb's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $71.93 billion.

For FY25, Wall Street analysts are expecting Airbnb to generate $12.06 billion in revenue, and if we assume a ~35% adjusted EBITDA margin on that revenue profile (the company is guiding to "at least 34.5%" margin this year), adjusted EBITDA would be $4.22 billion. This puts Airbnb's valuation at 17.0x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA.

On the bright side, Airbnb is trading slightly cheaper than Booking.com, which has been the investor favorite in the space for years. On the other hand, Expedia has not yet managed to shake off its sharp valuation discount since its One Key program rollout took longer than expected. Today, even though Expedia's growth has rebounded to similar levels as Airbnb, it's still trading much cheaper, which is why, on a net basis, I see more risk from a valuation standpoint for Airbnb.

All in all, I don't see any compelling reasons to buy Airbnb now, though I think the company's optimism for a bookings rebound in North America is encouraging. Monitor this stock from the sidelines for now.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Airbnb's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Airbnb's revenue grew 13% y/y to $3.10 billion, coming in well ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $3.04 billion (+10% y/y) by a three-point margin, while also accelerating sharply from just 6% growth in Q1.

More importantly, bookings were the core driver behind revenue acceleration. Bookings in Q2 grew 11% y/y to $22.5 billion, also accelerating four points versus 7% y/y growth in Q1. The acceleration in bookings growth, meanwhile, was driven primarily by ADR expansion of 3% y/y, while the actual number of nights booked grew at a 7% y/y pace that was roughly in line with Q1. This was driven by a shift toward short-term stays with higher rates, as well as the geographic mix as North American bookings rebounded.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Airbnb: the company reports bookings when customers place their reservations, but it records revenue when trips are taken. Since the start of the year, Airbnb has noted that customers' booking windows have shortened: that is, more and more people are taking last-minute trips instead of booking farther out in advance. This is a symptom of the broader economy, where consumers appear to prefer to wait (until deals appear, or until their financial situation becomes clearer) before booking a trip. The financial impact of this is that bookings and revenue, in nominal terms as well as in terms of y/y growth, are more likely to track together more closely.

As previously mentioned, one of the most encouraging signals that Airbnb offered this quarter was an improvement in bookings trends from North American travelers. The company noted as follows in the Q2 shareholder letter:

Following broader macro uncertainty earlier in the quarter, we saw an improvement in travel demand across North America. In the U.S., year-over-year growth of nights booked on a destination basis accelerated each month throughout Q2 2025, driven by domestic travel. Additionally, while travel from Canada to the U.S. remained soft in Q2 2025, total nights booked by Canadians–both within Canada and to destinations outside of North America accelerated from Q1 2025. These results continue to underscore the strategic benefit of our global diversification.

The company is expecting y/y growth trends in Nights and Seats booked to be similar in Q3 (as in Q2), with modest ADR expansion. To me, this is a hopeful indication that Airbnb will be able to hang on to a ~10% growth pace, though the outlook was poorly received by the markets.

On the profitability front, Airbnb capitalized on an improvement in its revenue take rates as well as a relatively modest increase in overhead to drive adjusted EBITDA 17% y/y higher to $1.04 billion, representing a 34% margin that improved one point y/y.

As a reminder, Airbnb's adjusted EBITDA margins were down in Q1, and on a full-year basis, the company's expectations for "at least 34.5%" margins are below 36.4% in FY24. This is an investment year for Airbnb as the company rolls out new features like Services and overhauls its app on top of a tough macroeconomy, but we're still hopeful that the company can continue on its trend of margin recovery in Q2 and exceed its margin targets for the year.

Key takeaways

Airbnb's Q2 results, and in particular its rebound in bookings and hopeful commentary on a pickup in bookings trends in North America, have me feeling incrementally more positive on the stock. That being said, Airbnb still trades at an uncomfortable premium to Expedia, and one quarter's worth of bookings re-acceleration doesn't mean Airbnb is safe in a tough macroeconomy. For now, continue to monitor this stock from the sidelines.

data.title: International Flavors & Fragrances Q2: A Good Business With A Bad Balance Sheet
data.description:
I’ve looked at International Flavors & Fragrances Inc. (NYSE:IFF) many times in the past few years, but it never quite made it to my watchlist. Not because I thought it was a bad company, but because IFF is one of those businesses that looks perfect at first glance with a solid brand, recurring demand, high gross margins, and a huge presence across industries that are part of all of our day-to-day life.

The problem is that every quarter, the numbers come in, there’s some cautious optimism from the bulls, maybe a little stock appreciation, and then the stock tanks back down. Over the last year, IFF has traded like a company stuck in a loop. And after going through their latest earnings, I think all of this is because the business itself is great, but the balance sheet is a mess.

Company and Earnings

If you are unsure about what the company does, the best explanation can be found on the main page of this company, located here on Seeking Alpha:

International Flavors & Fragrances Inc., together with its subsidiaries, manufactures and markets food, beverage, health and biosciences, scent, pharma solutions, and complementary adjacent products in the United States, Europe, and internationally. It operates in four segments Nourish, Health & Biosciences, Scent, and Pharma Solutions.

At first glance, Q2 2025 didn’t look bad at all. Sales were up slightly (1% year-over-year), gross margins expanded nicely to 36.2%, and adjusted EBITDA rose 6%. The company reaffirmed full-year guidance and finally brought its net leverage down to 2.5x, which, to their credit, is something they’ve been working hard to achieve.

The problem is that while operationally things are clearly improving, the bottom line is still not improving as much. On a (Non-GAAP) adjusted basis, IFF posted $185 million in net income, which is a solid number, no doubt. But when you compare it against $3 billion in quarterly revenue, you’re still looking at modest profitability. The bigger problem is free cash flow, which came in at just $94 million, which isn’t nearly enough to cover capex and dividends. So even with the income statement finally is in the black, the cash flow statement shows that everything isn’t so great yet.

But, at the same time, I really like IFF’s core business. This is the type of company we barely hear about, but they are responsible for making ingredients, flavors, enzymes, and scents for everything from food to cosmetics to pharmaceuticals, that we use all the time in completely different products. It can be in your toothpaste, your shampoo, your yogurt, your medicine. They sell to tens of thousands of customers in 65+ countries.

Segments

Their business is divided into segments, and the Taste business is the winner. In Q2, it posted a nice 6% sales gain driven by Latin America and Europe, following another solid quarter in Q1 with about 7% growth. It brought in $631 million in sales this quarter. EBITDA margin came in at 19.8%, which was slightly lower than a year ago (20.4% in Q2 2024).

Food Ingredients is also improving after a restructuring. This segment brought $850 million in net sales just this quarter. EBITDA margin rose to 14.6%, from 12.6% a year ago, which is great, as it means that the pricing actions are successful.

Scent showed a mixed picture with 1% growth in net sales, but 2% decline in operating EBITDA - Operating Margin shrank from 23.4% to 21.6% on a year-over-year basis. Fine Fragrance is growing (high single digits), but Fragrance Ingredients are still under pressure. Management is shifting the portfolio toward higher-value molecules, but that will take time. The same story goes for Health & Biosciences, once considered the high-margin growth machine, now in a soft patch due to weak demand and inventory destocking. It’s still the strongest segment in terms of operating margin (26.2% currently), but it declined from the same quarter last year (it was 26.8%).

The surprise winner this quarter was Pharma Solutions. It’s a small segment, just $103 million in sales this quarter, but it grew 21% year-over-year, making it IFF’s fastest-growing business by far. However, operating margin in this segment saw the steepest decline of all, falling from 25.3% to 21.4%. So while volume and demand are clearly surging, the business is becoming less profitable. That’s not necessarily a red flag, in high-growth phases, margins often compress, but it’s something worth watching.

So, IFF has strong segments and healthy operational margins, but all of this is not enough for the net income to be as impressive.

So Why Did The Stock Drop 6%?

At a certain point, the market’s patience starts to wear off, and this is probably what happened to IFF this quarter. IFF didn’t miss expectations this quarter, they didn’t even guide lower. But the stock still tanked because “it’s getting better” is just not enough anymore.

The financials aren’t terrible, but they’re not compelling, either. Free cash flow is positive, about $94 million year-to-date, but it’s not enough to fund both internal investment and dividend payments. Operating cash flow is higher, around $368 million, which helps, but it still doesn’t show the serious turnaround everyone hopes to see.

And then there’s the balance sheet. Gross debt is down significantly, which is great, but we’re still looking at $6.2 billion in debt and just $816 million in cash. That’s a lot of pressure for a business that’s still rebuilding confidence. Don’t get me wrong, IFF can pay its bills, but this isn’t the kind of setup that gets investors excited to buy the stock.

Besides, the management has made it clear that 2025 is a transition year. The big recovery is not expected to show up until 2026. So, Wall Street is looking at a stock with no clear near-term catalyst, soft free cash flow, and a business model that’s still digesting past mistakes.

We have strengthening commercial and R&D pipelines that I am confident will begin to show more impact in 2026 and build for full benefit in 2027.

We are clear on our strategy, are getting stronger at commercial innovation, production and productivity execution, and we'll get through this as we build strength for 2026 and beyond.

By the way, the market’s reaction was twofold. Investors sold the news first, then realized the worst didn’t happen, and maybe even a few good things are going to get out of it, so the stock started its recovery after the market closed. After all, IFF reiterated full-year guidance, leverage is improved significantly, and the company announced a $500 million share repurchase plan. It could have been enough to attract some buyers, especially quant funds.

Valuation: It’s Not Cheap

You might think that with all this drama, IFF would be trading like a distressed asset. Well, at least this is what I expected to see pressing on the valuation tab. But it’s not. The stock sits around 16.5x forward earnings (on an adjusted basis), and almost 3x forward PEG (price to earnings divided by growth). That’s higher than you’d expect for a company with financial struggles.

Gross margins are high, but the return on equity is negative. Free cash flow margin is still in the red. The fact that IFF is investing and restructuring, together with paying interest, really hurts its bottom line. To me, this is a stock that looks "not terrible," but isn't priced for the level of risk it still carries. It's not expensive, but it’s definitely not screaming “deep value.”

What Needs to Happen Next

At this point, the path forward isn’t rocket science. IFF knows what it needs to do, and it seems like the market knows what to expect. First, they have to keep pushing margins higher in the Taste and Food Ingredients segments. That’s where the pricing power is, and where the company still has a chance to build consistency. Then there’s Health & Biosciences, the segment that’s still a bit messy. The restructuring is well underway, but volume recovery isn’t there yet.

Debt is another problem, even though the leverage is coming down. Their balance sheet really needs a reduction in total debt, because interest expense is still eating up too much of the cash flow. And speaking of cash flow, they need more of it. At least enough to pay the dividend.

Conclusion

IFF is a good company. I honestly believe that. The problem is that, for the past few years, it was run more like a private equity firm than a public company. Now they’re trying to fix it, and they’ve made some progress. But unless you're incredibly patient, I don't think this is the perfect stock to invest in. There is no clear upside catalyst until late 2025 or 2026, and until then, I would stay aside. Especially, it’s smart because the stock is in a negative momentum.

data.title: Rivian Q2: Another Quarter Of Disappointment
data.description:
Rivian: Challenges Galore...

Well, it looks like I should have persevered with my past bearish rating on the stock of Rivian Automotive, Inc. (NASDAQ:RIVN), but I had higher hopes that its execution could improve. Yet, somehow management demonstrated why the woes of the subscale EV manufacturer is far from over, pressured by President Trump's less generous regulatory regime on EVs, while also being hampered by the current rare earth export control issues with China. Hence, the EV maker's efforts to make critical capacity adjustments to prime the production for its lower-priced R2 platform has faced an untimely snafu.

In my previous RIVN writeup, I raised several persistent challenges that have exacted a frustrating toll on the bullish sentiments of the stock. While the market appears to have reflected its relatively tepid deliveries/production scorecard in early July, the market probably realized that the flow of bad news may not quite have ended yet.

Nevertheless, Rivian believes that its profitability trajectory toward 2027 shouldn't be materially affected. Yet, the need for management to lower its optimism for FY2025 has offered investors a sobering admonition about the high ambiguities concerning Rivian's near-term outlook. As a reminder, Rivian posted an automotive gross loss of $335M, emanating from the fixed cost under absorption at its current significantly underutilized Illinois facility (plausibly no more than 20% utilization based on nameplate capacity).

Rivian's Profitability Roadmap Faces A Murky Outlook

Hence, the less than 6K in units produced for Q2 really hampered the company's near-term profitability performance, resulting in an impact of $14K per unit, while also needing to account for higher LCNRV related costs in the same quarter. With that in mind, I'm not surprised that management decided to rein in expectations on Rivian's 2025 adjusted EBITDA outlook, while still needing to carry out retooling work in Q3 to prime for R2 manufacturing.

Note that the revised guidance indicates a range of between -$2B and -$2.5B in adjusted EBITDA, a decline from the previous range of between -$1.7B and -$1.9B. Also, with the significant reduction in regulatory credits revenue growth prospects, the market must also consider whether Rivian's gross margins accretion could be further affected since these aren't cyclical headwinds.

I think herein lies the problem for subscale EV makers like Rivian. Of course, we know that the entry-level price of $45K for R2 should theoretically expand access to a broader customer segment, but also competing more aggressively with Tesla (TSLA). And although the EV transformation plans by the legacy automakers are assessed to have been scaled back, even Tesla's recent performance shows that the overall market environment is expected to remain challenging.

Hence, even with a lower price point, it doesn't "magically" help Rivian gain to bolster demand dynamics significantly, justifying the increase in production footprint for R2. Note that Rivian has earmarked 155K units for R2 production. However, I believe astute investors comprehend that Rivian's underutilization has cost it dearly in terms of fixed costs leverage, hampering the accuracy in its gross profits forecasts.

Still the company indicates that it retains robust support from Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTC:VLKPF) as a key financing partner and investor. Yet, I think the market will increasingly focus on execution moving ahead, even if the liquidity challenges are less arduous as compared to the days it had to navigate with Volkswagen liquidity injection.

With more than $7.5B in current cash and equivalents (not including a further $2.5B in incremental capital from Volkswagen, and the possible $6.6B DoE loan), I don't think Rivian is in an imminent liquidity crisis that could have caused even more consternation on the part of investors.

Rivian's Cash Burn Must Be Monitored Closely

Despite that, Rivian is still estimated to burn through $8.4B in cash flow between 2025-27. Hence, as long as the incremental milestones agreed with Volkswagen are met, it seems like the company might just scrape through, if it could improve upon its production/deliveries metrics from 2026.

But, that remains a big if for now, as the sales cadence for R2 remains untested, while the industry is circumventing cyclical headwinds that have impacted the overall EV market. And the tariff-related impacts might still percolate through Rivian's P&L over the next couple of years, although management emphasized that the planning process in the second half should be less murky as compared to the first half.

Yet, we have already observed just how challenging it's to make accurate prognosis, amidst the current challenges hampering Rivian's supply chain. Hence, Rivian investors seeking more clarity into its production cadence from 2026 might conceivably not have the answers they need until the latter part of this year.

Buy Or Sell RIVN Stock?

With that in mind, I don't expect RIVN buyers to return with the level of conviction that saw the stock re-test the $17 level in May 2025. As seen above, the stock has since collapsed from that peak, with this week's selloff also eclipsing the July lows. Unprofitable EV stocks like RIVN has had a very grueling year in 2025, dealing with the policy flipflops of the Trump administration, while steering past frustrating industry challenges and supply chain snag.

And, the market has little "sympathy" for poor performance from growth-related plays like RIVN. But, that's how it should be. We are all here to invest profitably, and Rivian has yet to convince the market that it can accelerate its route to profitability (in contrast, it seems to have backtracked with its lowered guidance).

With the stock inching closer to a possible support zone at the $10.5 level, keeping my hold rating seems apt, while we allow the market action to pan out.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VPLS: Beyond The Core In Fixed Income
data.description:
Introduction

In today's article, we will analyse the Vanguard Core-Plus Bond ETF (NASDAQ:VPLS) exchange-traded fund investment vehicle, which operates under the umbrella of the investment management company Vanguard. This fund is actively managed and seeks to provide an acceptable level of return while generating steady cash flows, operating in the US fixed income market.

Its benchmark is the Bloomberg U.S. Universal Total Return Index, which includes government bonds, corporate bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and some foreign debt. The fund managers use active management (‘core-plus’), which starts with allocating the fund's capital to treasuries or investment-grade corporate bonds as the “core” part of the strategy, leaving the ‘plus’ for high-yield bonds with lower credit quality, thereby seeking extra returns. In addition to this, managers often use derivatives, such as interest rate futures or swaps, to adjust the fund's various positions and improve both its exposure and risk management.

Its exposure range is 80% in treasury bonds or investment-grade bonds under normal market conditions, leaving a ceiling of 20% of the total capital managed by the fund for investments in high-yield bonds. The fund earns money either through monthly coupon payments on the bonds in its portfolio or through the appreciation of these bonds over time. The main risks associated with this investment would be interest rate risk, as a change in the yield curve would alter the NAV and the fund's return accordingly. Credit risk, caused by widening or rating downgrades, and liquidity risk, as mortgage securities are less liquid and may incur higher trading costs.

In the following images, the reader will find two images showing the main characteristics of the fund and its distribution broken down by instrument and credit quality. As the reader can see, as of 30 June this year, the vehicle analysed has 1,808 securities in its portfolio, compared to the more than 20,000 held by its benchmark index. This could initially appear to be a red flag if it were a passively managed vehicle, but as the fund managers actively manage the fund, we do not consider it to be a cause for concern. The vehicle has sufficient securities to be well diversified and achieve its target return.

Its average effective maturity is 8.6 years, compared to 8.0 years in the index, while its duration is 5.7 years (5.8 years in the benchmark). This average duration places the fund in the ‘medium’ range of the interest rate sensitivity scale, balancing the potential for gains in the event of falling interest rates with protection against moderate increases.

45.7% of assets are invested in US Treasury debt and federal agencies. The remaining 46.6% is divided between investment grade (AAA–BBB totalling 41.6%) and below-investment grade credit (BB–CCC, with 9.8%). Only 0.6% falls into CCC or below, which gives clues as to the caution of the management team in expanding exposure to high yield in order to achieve that aforementioned ‘plus’ in returns. Finally, in this section, it should be noted that its turnover rate reaches 471.6%, reflecting the intensity of active management and constant adjustment to changing credit and interest rate conditions.

Analysis – Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyse some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The first metric presented is the implied forward rates of the Treasury curve. This reflects market expectations regarding future interest rates at different maturity intervals. The graph shows lines such as ‘1 M→3 M’, ‘3 M→6 M’, and ‘1 Y→2 Y’, which indicate the rate at which financing can be agreed today for those future horizons. As of today, the slope of the short maturities (1 M→3 M and 3 M→6 M) has been declining from highs in May 2024 of around 5.5% to levels of around 4.3%, while the long maturities (5 Y→10 Y and 10 Y→30 Y) are around 4.6–5.1%, following a recent small rebound.

This relative flattening of the forward curve implies that the market is discounting rate cuts in the short term but remains more cautious about longer maturities. For a manager such as the one of the funds analysed, these signals serve as a guide for adjusting the effective duration: when the medium-long segment offers a higher carry and the market does not foresee sharp rises, increasing the duration in these areas somewhat can bring price gains and a higher implied coupon, but as we will see in the next metric, it may not be wise to do so without any measure, as we will see in the next indicator.

The second metric we analyse is the z-score of the exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA) of parameter β₁ of the Nelson-Siegel curve, which measures the slope of the yield curve. In practice, β₁ reflects how steep the curve is between short and long maturities; its change tells us whether the curve is steepening or flattening relative to its own past history.

As of today, this z-score is slightly below zero (around −0.5), after having gone through a very broad cycle: from peaks of around +3 in the first months of 2023—when the curve rose sharply after anticipating increases—to lows of almost −2 in early 2022 and again in early 2025. The current moderate negative value indicates that, in recent weeks, the slope has been flattening somewhat faster than usual, although without reaching market extremes. For fund managers, this signal is key when adjusting the portfolio structure based on the yield curve. This negative Z-score may suggest that it is no longer worthwhile to seek capital gains via steepening by overweighting the long end of the curve relative to the short end.

Therefore, managers could slightly reduce exposure to the medium- to long-term portion of the curve, offsetting this by opening positions in the short-term segment or adjusting the effective duration of the vehicle through the use of derivatives.

The third indicator shows, using a heat map of z-scores in 21-day windows, how credit spreads, term, liquidity, and high-yield spreads have performed relative to their historical average. Each coloured box reflects how many standard deviations the current spread is from its recent average: red colours indicate significant widening and blue colours indicate compression in the different spreads.

As of today, the different z-scores are around a positive value of 0.5 for high yield and corporate credit segments. For a fund of this nature, this moderate spread environment offers two interpretations. On the one hand, the extra coupon that managers obtain by overweighting credit remains attractive compared to the coupon currently paid by treasuries. In this environment, and with no signs of widening in the near future, we could conclude that it would be interesting to rotate part of the capital into credit bonds to capitalise on that extra coupon.

Conclusion

In conclusion, and to wrap up this article, I recommend keeping the analysed vehicle in your portfolio for several reasons. Forward rates point to an increase in the medium to long term, which could justify an increase in duration to capture that extra income. However, on the other hand, the second indicator shows that the curve is flattening faster than usual, which reduces the potential extra income that can be captured every day. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Schlumberger: Cash Flowing Through The Cycle Bottom
data.description:
Investment thesis

SLB, formerly Schlumberger (NYSE:SLB), isn't having a good year and is down 20% from January when I last reviewed it for Seeking Alpha. While broader energy services ETFs (XES) are also down, SLB has lagged behind Baker Hughes (BKR), a peer with less upstream exposure, and TechnipFMC (FTI), a subsea niche player with significant backlog:

Although the current oil bear market started in 2022, SLB's business model was seen as relatively resilient by analysts due to its low exposure to the volatile, short-cycle oilfield capex in the US. Long-cycle, international markets where SLB gets 80% of its revenue are considered more robust to oil price fluctuations.

However, over the last 12-18 months international oil producers have also cut spending, particularly in the Middle East (Saudi Aramco) and LATAM (Pemex). Consequently, SLB delivered lower numbers compared to Q2 2024. Even if this wasn't unexpected - the earnings were nominally a "beat" - the earnings call didn't suggest an imminent recovery in oilfield spend either.

On the brighter side and unlike prior downturns, SLB continues to generate ample free cash flow (or FCF) at about ~9% yield, implying the market is pricing in close to zero future growth. Energy services may indeed stagnate until 2026 or 2027, but long-term growth is virtually guaranteed by the 6-7% annual depletion rate for the average oilfield.

In the meantime, the 9% FCF yield covers the 3.4% dividend plus the share buybacks. SLB's dividend has also placed it among the holdings of the popular Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). The shareholder returns alone may make SLB a reasonable investment to hold in flat energy services environment while awaiting the repricing once the cycle ultimately turns around.

SLB's recent performance was probably impacted too by the 15 months it took to close the ChampionX acquisition due to regulatory hurdles in the UK. The acquisition is fundamentally positive as it shifts SLB's revenue mix from oilfield "capex" (developing new oil production) to "opex" (maintaining existing production) and reduces the earnings volatility. The synergies guided by management aren't insignificant either.

Strategically, I continue to see SLB as a "buy." E&P customers may shift their spending timeline "to the right" but ultimately the longer capex is deferred, the bigger the depletion gap that will need to be filled. Tactically, though, it appears we may retest the April 9 lows, so it may be prudent to wait for the market's confirmation we aren't headed for a lower low. Currently I maintain long exposure through sold puts.

Q2 showed weakness in International

The energy services paradigm has shifted from 2023. Two years ago, the slowdown was in the US while International was still growing. However, as SLB's earnings show, the Middle East and Latin America are now also experiencing headwinds, despite the long-cycle nature of their production:

In fact, North America was (relatively) the best performing region year-on-year.

Worldwide, EBITDA appears to have peaked for now but it remains on par with 2023-2024:

EBITDA margins are also down though still high historically:

Unfortunately for SLB, the price-to-earnings multiple, which was factoring in quite a bit of growth, has now collapsed:

It now sits at a less aspirational 10-11x level. As a high-level calculation, if we assume SLB faces a 10% cost of capital, we could say the 10x multiple - equivalent to 10% earnings yield - prices in zero growth. The FCF yield indicates a similar conclusion.

CEO Le Peuch's Q2 comments implied a flat to low single digit second-half growth, so if that holds, the valuation correction should be close to done:

[To] be coming back to the guidance, I think the guidance reflects from the low to the high guidance, flat to LSD with potential to go even beyond this.

However, the call didn't appear to provide any clear indication either when SLB expects international growth to resume. For example, regarding the more resilient subsea and offshore markets Mr. Le Peuch commented:

[To] comment on what we have seen for the last 12 to 18 months. We have seen white space developing. And indeed, the least critical project to shift to the right. but we still have seen the rich pipeline of advantaged projects that are key for the portfolio of international operators, partly the IOCs to be going forward with anticipated FID.

"Shift to the right" refers to projects being postponed. While this is much better than projects being canceled altogether and the opportunity pipeline still looks good, there is no guarantee these projects won't be postponed yet again. In other words, while SLB management reiterated its long-term confidence regarding international markets, they didn't visibly commit to promising a sharp or "V-shaped" recovery.

My personal view though is the longer an "L-shaped" recovery takes, the sharper the capex rebound would ultimately be. A longer recovery also tends to favor companies that are cash positive at the cyclical lows and can wait out the downturn.

Key international markets may have bottomed

Baker Hughes' (BKR) widely tracked international rig count aligns to SLB's results. As of Q2 and apart from the US, the weakest markets were Latin America and the Middle East:

In Latin America, Mexico has been a source of weakness as the heavily indebted national oil company PEMEX has lacked the means to finance new developments.

Mexico's government, however, doesn't seem pleased with the oil production decline that has resulted from deferring investment. The government just recently, through a complex financial operation, effectively injected $12 billion into the company, and there are also plans to spur more investment by farming out stakes to private operators.

Since February-March Mexico's rig count is up 50% so perhaps the lows are behind us:

In the Middle East, Saudi Aramco has been reducing investment since it publicly gave up on its ambition to increase its "maximum sustainable capacity" for oil production from 12 to 13 million barrels per day. The shift in Aramco's plans also resulted in a large number of rig suspensions, both offshore and land, which reduces demand for SLB's services.

More recently, though, there are indications some Saudi rigs are coming back online. Overall, the Saudi rig count may be trying to stabilize around 70:

Outside of the US, Saudi Arabia and LATAM, management seemed fairly optimistic:

Regionally, the Middle East and Asia will continue to display the most resilience in the short term, driven by lower break-evens and a sustained focus on energy security. Meanwhile, advantaged offshore projects will lend support to the steady market across Europe, Africa and Americas. In contrast, land activity across North America and Latin America have the greatest downside risk due to short cycle spend.

Source: Management comments from Q2 call

The ChampionX acquisition has now closed

The long awaited close of the ChampionX acquisition may also be a catalyst. ChampionX, itself the product of a not so distant merger between Apergy and Ecolab's (ECL) energy upstream business, specializes in production chemicals and artificial lift technology. Unlike the drilling or completions businesses which depend on upstream capex, ChampionX's portfolio is geared towards production enhancement and optimization, which fall under operating expenditures.

The upstream energy business is notoriously capex intensive, with energy companies having to spend huge amounts of money to drill and complete wells. However, once the oil or gas is flowing, the capex is already a sunk cost and the operating expenses required to maintain production are much smaller in comparison. When faced with low oil prices, upstream companies may be quick to cut down on new capex, but they almost never shut existing production which makes upstream opex less volatile.

SLB's long-term view appears to be that oilfield capex will peak in the next 15-20 years, after which upstream spending would be dominated by opex:

With ChampionX, SLB is trying to position its portfolio for the coming changes:

Customers are in a quest to unlock and optimize the full production potential of their assets while improving efficiency in the reservoir recovery phase of their operations. This is creating a less cyclical and growing market opportunity that is more opex driven and is less sensitive to short-term commodity cycles.

Source: Management comments from Q2 call

In the near term, there is of course management's promise of non-trivial synergies which was reiterated on the Q2 call:

We are now even more comfortable with our initial assessment that we will be able to generate $400 million of annual pretax synergies within the first 3 years after closing.

SLB will start consolidating ChampionX into its results in August 2025, so Q3 will be the first quarter reflective of the combined business. As management's ability to provide guidance on combined basis has been limited, I would expect more significant revisions to analysts estimates once the restrictions are no longer in place.

The shareholder returns continue

SLB's shareholder returns have been resilient despite the industry-wide malaise in energy services:

In 2024, SLB spent $1.8 billion on share repurchases and paid out $1.5 billion in dividends, for a total distribution yield of ~7%.

So far in 2025, SLB has devoted $2.4 billion to repurchases, already exceeding its 2024 total. Per management's Q2 comments, more than half of the 141 million shares issued to finance the ChampionX acquisition have been offset:

Since the announcement of the ChampionX transaction in April 2024, we have been accelerating the repurchasing of our shares. To that end, since the announcement, we have reduced our total shares outstanding by 78 million. This represents 55% of the shares we just issued in this transaction.

The dividends continue at $0.285 per quarter, an increase of 3.6% from the $0.275 rate in 2024. The forward dividend yield is about 3.4% now. Assuming no further repurchases for the remainder of the year and ignoring the dividend on the incremental ChampionX shares, SLB would end 2025 with $4 billion returned, reflecting a ~8% total distribution yield.

Importantly, SLB's returns are 1) well covered by its FCF generation; 2) also covered at what is a low point for the oilfield services cycle. Analyst consensus implies a 9% FCF yield:

Management emphasized that capex will stay at 5% to 7% of revenue for the near term as the company remains mindful of its FCF conversion goals.

Equity analysts see significant potential

The price targets continue to slide but analysts seem to be increasing the implied upside percentage, now at 36%:

Frankly, the analyst bullishness on SLB with 19 of 29 ratings being "strong buy" worries me a bit as "too consensus":

SLB is indeed the largest player in the space, but energy services stocks rarely get so much "love" from the Street, especially in what has been a lackluster year for oil prices.

Over the next few quarters, I expect analysts to look for "tangible" evidence the promised ChampionX synergies are being realized. That may influence how these price targets evolve in the near term.

Risks to watch out for

The OPEC+ spare capacity remains a risk but probably less so as the "voluntary cuts" have pretty much been reversed. However, oil prices can also be pressured by demand weakness in the event the global economy slows down.

I am also cautious of the risk that upstream FIDs (final investment decisions) slide further "to the right", meaning that projects expected to commence in 2026 are postponed to 2027-2028. This could be driven not just by oil prices but also the general economic uncertainty, including tariffs, or supply chain bottlenecks as seen in the offshore segment where FPSOs (floating production storage and offloading units) faced some delays.

Takeaway

Despite the disappointing macro environment for energy services, SLB remains a high-quality company that is able to reward its shareholders even through the cyclical bottom. The downside is likely limited from here while the upside, when upstream capex inevitably returns, could be considerable.
